lesson:
氏し (family name/clan) + 名めい (given name) = full name. But you'll almost never hear this word in conversation. 氏名しめい lives on forms, documents, official applications, and bureaucratic instructions. It's the "full legal name" version of asking who you are.

If you fill out any paperwork in Japan — at a hotel, a bank, a government office, a doctor — you'll see 氏名しめい printed next to a blank line. That's where your full name goes. Sometimes the form separates it into 氏し (family name) and 名めい (given name) on separate lines. In Japan, family name comes first — always.

In casual life, you'd use 名前なまえ (なまえ) for "name." 氏名しめい is strictly for forms and official contexts. Knowing this word is less about conversation and more about literacy — recognizing it when you see it on paper.

今こん (now/this) + 夜や (night) = tonight. The 今こん compound machine keeps working: 今日きょう (today), 今週しゅうこんしゅう (this week), 今月つきこんげつ (this month), 今年としことし (this year), and now 今夜こんや (tonight). Same pattern every time.

The reading こんや uses Chinese-derived こん for 今こん (like 今週しゅうこんしゅう, 今月つきこんげつ) and や for 夜や. Compare with 今晩こんばん (こんばん, this evening) — which you already know from こんばんは (good evening). The two are nearly interchangeable, but 今夜こんや leans slightly more toward late night while 今晩こんばん covers the broader evening.

今夜よるこんやは何をする？ (what are you doing tonight?) — a simple, high-frequency question.

夜よ (night) + 中なか (middle) = the middle of the night. Not midnight on the dot (that's 零時れいじ or 十じゅう二に時とき), but the general dead-of-night zone — roughly midnight to 3 or 4 AM, when the world is asleep. 夜よる中なかに目めが覚おぼめた (I woke up in the middle of the night).

夜中よなか carries a mood. It implies darkness, quiet, a time when you're not supposed to be awake. 夜よる中なかにうるさい (loud in the middle of the night) is a complaint. 夜よる中なかまで勉強つよべんきょうした (studied until the middle of the night) implies exhausting effort. Things that happen 夜よる中なかに are either desperate, illicit, or the mark of serious dedication.

The 中なか here works the same way as in 昼ひる間ま (ひるま, daytime — the middle of the day-period). It pins you to the core of a time span, not its edges.

来らい (come/next) + 週しゅう (week) = the coming week. The 来らい prefix is the future counterpart to 先さき (last) and 今いま (this): 先週せんしゅう → 今週こんしゅう → 来週らいしゅう. Last week, this week, next week.

The same 来らい pattern scales up: 来月つきらいげつ (らいげつ, next month), 来年としらいねん (らいねん, next year). And going backward, 先さき handles the past: 先週せんしゅう, 先月せんげつ. This three-part time system is completely regular — one of the few things in Japanese you can just slot in mechanically.

来週しゅうらいしゅうの月つき曜日にち (next Monday). 来週しゅうらいしゅうまでに (by next week). 来週しゅうらいしゅうはどう？ (how about next week?). No particles needed after 来週らいしゅう when it's a time reference — it works as a bare time noun, just like 今日にちきょう or 明日にちあした.

You already know the rule from いつ: question word + か = indefinite. いつか = someday. どこか = somewhere. And now: 誰だれか = someone. The か particle strips the question-ness away and replaces it with vagueness — you're no longer asking "who?" but referring to an unspecified person.

誰だれかいる？ (is someone there?). 誰だれかに聞きいて (ask someone). 誰だれか来くた (someone came). Notice that particles like が are often dropped after 誰だれか in casual speech — 誰だれかが来くた and 誰だれか来くた both work, but the second is more natural in conversation.

Keep building your question-word transformation table: 誰だれか (someone), 誰だれも + negative (no one), 誰だれでも (anyone). Same pattern as every other question word. Once you have it for one, you have it for all of them.

You already know 何なん read as なに — "what." This entry is for the other reading: なん. Same kanji, same meaning, but used in different phonetic contexts. The rule is roughly: use なん before で, の, and だ/です, and before counters. Use なに everywhere else.

何なんですか (なんですか, what is it?) — なん because です follows. 何なんで (なんで, why? / by what means?) — なん because で follows. 何人なんにん (なんにん, how many people?) — なん before a counter. But: 何なにを食たべる？ (なにをたべる, what will you eat?) — なに because を follows.

The distinction is phonetic convenience — なに before で would create a clumsy なにで, while なんで flows better. Your ear will pick this up quickly; it's one of those things that sounds wrong before you can explain why.

今朝けさ is one of those words where the reading has nothing to do with the individual kanji readings. 今こん is normally いま or こん. 朝あさ is normally あさ or ちょう. But together? けさ. No trace of either reading. This is a 熟字じ訓 (jukujikun) — a compound where the meaning is right (now + morning = this morning) but the reading is a separate native Japanese word plastered on top of the kanji. けさ is an ancient Japanese word for "this morning" that predates the kanji assignment.

You've already seen this phenomenon: 今日にちきょう (きょう), 今年としことし (ことし), 昨日にちきのう (きのう), 明日にちあした (あした). Time words are especially prone to irregular readings because the Japanese time vocabulary is old — older than the kanji system they borrowed from Chinese. The native words refused to be replaced.

今朝あさけさは寒さむかった (it was cold this morning). No particles needed — bare time noun, like all the others.

The kanji 寿司すし are 当あて字じ (ateji) — characters chosen for their sounds, not their meanings. 寿じゅ means "longevity" and 司し means "to manage/govern." Neither has anything to do with vinegared rice. They were picked because they sound like すし and happen to look auspicious. You'll also see sushi written as 鮨すし (fish + delicious) or 鮓すし, which are older and more etymologically honest. But 寿司すし won the popularity contest because those "longevity" associations made it perfect for celebrations.

The word すし originally referred to the preservation method — fish pressed into fermented rice, left for months. The quick, fresh sushi you know (握り寿司, にぎりずし, hand-pressed sushi) is a relatively recent Tokyo invention from the early 1800s. Street food that went upscale.

寿司を食たべに行いこう (let's go eat sushi). Note the construction: 〜に行く (go in order to do something). This is the sentence that starts many good evenings.

meaning: to wear (below the waist), put on (below the waist)
lesson:
Japanese splits "to wear" into different verbs based on what body part the clothing goes on. 着きる (きる) is for upper body — shirts, jackets, dresses. 被かぶる (かぶる) is for the head — hats, helmets. And 履はく is for everything below the waist: pants, skirts, shoes, socks. ズボンを履はく (put on pants). 靴くつを履はく (put on shoes). 靴下くつしたを履はく (put on socks).

The dividing line is roughly the waist. If it goes on your legs or feet, 履はく. This verb doesn't exist in English — we just "put on" everything regardless — so it takes conscious practice. The mental model: 履はく involves stepping into something or sliding it up your legs.

The kanji 履り contains 尸しかばね (body) and 復ふく (repeat/return), suggesting repeated steps — feet in motion, feet in footwear. It's the same kanji in 履り歴書か (りれきしょ, resume — literally a "record of footsteps").

Particles: 履はく takes を — the garment or footwear is your direct object. 靴くつを履はく, ジーンズを履りく.

Potential が履りける: が — what you can wear becomes the subject. このサイズの靴くつが履ける (I can wear shoes this size).

Passive に履りかれる: rare — clothing isn't usually the agent. But: スリッパを履りかれてしまった (someone wore my slippers — and now I'm annoyed).

Causative に…を履りかせる: に marks who you dress, を marks what. 子供こどもに靴くつを履かせる (put shoes on the kid).

五段 Godan他動詞
Dictionaryはくを
Negativeはかないを
Politeはきますを
Polite negはきませんを
Pastはいたを
Polite pastはきましたを
て-formはいてを
Volitionalはこうを
Polite volはきましょうを
Passiveはかれるを
Causativeはかせるに／を
Caus-passはかせられるに
Potentialはけるが
Imperativeはけを
Imp なさいはきなさいを
Cond ～ばはけばを
Cond ～たらはいたらを

おじさん means uncle — or more broadly, any middle-aged man. Just as English might use "mister" loosely, Japanese uses おじさん for any man who looks roughly 40-60. The kanji, when written, depends on the relationship: 伯父おじさん for your parent's older brother, 叔父おじさん for your parent's younger brother. In speech, both are just おじさん.

The length trap again: おじさん (uncle, short じ) vs. おじいさん (grandfather, long じい). One extra mora is the difference between middle-aged and elderly. This is the same pair from the おじいさん lesson, now seen from the other side. Get the vowel length wrong and you're accidentally calling someone ancient — or weirdly young.

When talking about your own uncle to someone outside your family, the humble form is おじ — drop the さん. Same in-group/out-group rule as all Japanese family terms.

The female counterpart to おじさん. おばさん means aunt or any middle-aged woman. The kanji split: 伯母おばさん for your parent's older sister, 叔母おばさん for your parent's younger sister. Spoken, both are おばさん.

And the same vowel-length minefield applies: おばさん (aunt/middle-aged woman, short ば) vs. おばあさん (grandmother/elderly woman, long ばあ). Getting this wrong is socially risky in exactly the same way as the おじ/おじい pair. Japanese people are acutely aware of this distinction, and calling a woman おばあさん when she considers herself おばさん will not go over well.

In contemporary Japanese, some women have started to reclaim or joke about the おばさん label — おばさん力りょく (おばさんりょく, "auntie power") refers to the boldness and social fearlessness that stereotypically comes with middle age. The word is getting a cultural upgrade.

The grandmother counterpart to おじいさん. おばあさん is someone else's grandmother, or the word you use addressing your own grandmother directly. When referring to your own grandmother in conversation with outsiders, use the humble 祖母そぼ (そぼ).

The word also applies to any elderly woman — おばあさん at the neighborhood shop, おばあさん in a fairy tale. Japanese folklore is full of おじいさん and おばあさん pairs — 昔々むかしむかし、おじいさんとおばあさんがいました (once upon a time, there lived an old man and an old woman) is the standard opening for traditional stories, the Japanese equivalent of "once upon a time."

The four-level system is now complete: おばさん (aunt/middle-aged) → おばあさん (grandmother/elderly). おじさん (uncle/middle-aged) → おじいさん (grandfather/elderly). Short vowel = younger generation. Long vowel = older generation. Four words, one vowel-length rule.

いとこ is one of those words that can be written with an absurd number of kanji combinations — 従兄弟いとこ, 従姉妹いとこ, 従兄いとこ, 従妹いもうと, and more, depending on whether the cousin is male or female, older or younger. In practice, almost everyone just writes it in hiragana: いとこ. The kanji options exist but they're a headache nobody wants.

Japanese cousin terminology reflects a culture that cares about relative age. Technically, an older male cousin is 従兄いとこ (じゅうけい), a younger female cousin is 従妹いもうと (じゅうまい), and so on. But spoken Japanese just uses いとこ for everyone, regardless of gender or age — the simplification happened naturally because the formal terms were too unwieldy for conversation.

The 従じゅう in the kanji means "follow/accompany" — a cousin follows alongside your direct family line without being part of it. Etymologically neat, practically ignored.

辞じ (word/expression) + 書しょ (book/writing) = word-book. A dictionary. The kanji 辞じ is specifically about language and vocabulary — you'll see it in 辞典 (じてん, dictionary — slightly more formal) and 辞める (やめる, to resign — originally "to decline with words").

辞書 vs 辞典 vs 字典: All mean "dictionary" but with different scopes. 辞書じしょ is the general everyday word. 辞典 sounds more formal, more comprehensive — an encyclopedia-style reference. 字じ典 (じてん, same pronunciation) is specifically a kanji dictionary. In daily speech, 辞書じしょ covers everything. 辞書かじしょで調しらべる (look it up in the dictionary) — the sentence every language learner lives by.

Electronic dictionaries (電でん子こ辞書かじしょ) were once ubiquitous in Japan — dedicated handheld devices that every student carried. Smartphones have largely replaced them, but you'll still see the term and occasionally the devices in schools.

朝あさ (morning) + ご飯はん (cooked rice / meal) = morning meal. Breakfast. The pattern is dead simple and produces all three meals: 朝あさご飯はん (breakfast), 昼ひるご飯 (ひるごはん, lunch), 晩ばんご飯 (ばんごはん, dinner). Morning rice, noon rice, evening rice. Three words for the price of one pattern.

The ご in ご飯はん is an honorific prefix — even rice gets polite treatment. Dropping it (朝あさ飯, あさめし) sounds rougher, more masculine, more casual. Same meal, different social register.

A traditional Japanese 朝あさご飯はん looks nothing like a Western breakfast: rice, miso soup, grilled fish, pickled vegetables, a raw egg, natto (fermented soybeans). No pancakes, no cereal. Knowing 朝あさご飯はん without knowing what it typically contains is missing half the picture.

You already know 白しろい (しろい, the adjective "white"). This entry is 白しろ the noun — the color white as a thing. 白しろが好すき (I like white). 白しろにする (I'll go with white). When you're choosing a color — for a car, a phone case, a t-shirt — you name the noun form.

Japanese color words have this adjective/noun split for the traditional colors: 白しろい/白しろ, 黒くろい/黒くろ, 赤あかい/赤あか, 青あおい/青あお. The adjective describes something (白しろい猫ねこ, a white cat). The noun names the color itself (白しろ, white).

In Japanese culture, 白しろ is the color of purity, death, and new beginnings simultaneously. Wedding kimonos are white. Funeral offerings use white. Blank paper is a white beginning. The color carries a weight that the English "white" doesn't quite match — it's closer to "blank" or "clean slate" than to any single symbolism.

どっち is the casual contraction of どちら — "which one" when choosing between two things. どっちがいい？ (which one do you want?). どっちでもいい (either is fine). The っ gives it a snappy, informal punch that どちら doesn't have.

The full set: こっち (this way/mine), そっち (that way/yours), あっち (over there), どっち (which way?). These are the ko-so-a-do directional pronouns in casual form. Use them with friends, family, anyone you're comfortable with. Switch to こちら/そちら/あちら/どちら for politeness.

どっち only works for two options. For three or more, you need どれ. This two-vs-many distinction doesn't exist in casual English ("which one" works for any number), but Japanese insists on it. Two choices: どっち. Three or more: どれ.

The casual version of そちら — "that way" or "your side." Where こっち is my zone, そっち is your zone. そっちはどう？ (how is it over there? / how about on your end?). そっちに行いく (I'll go to where you are).

On the phone, そっち is especially useful. When you can't see the other person, space becomes abstract — そっち means "your situation, your side of things." そっちの天気てんきはどう？ (how's the weather there?). そっちは忙いそがしい？ (are you busy on your end?).

Remember the register split: そっち for casual, そちら for polite. Using そっち with your boss would be like saying "your end" instead of "if I may ask about your situation" — technically the same meaning, but the casualness stands out.

明めい (bright) + 日にち (day) = bright day, tomorrow. The kanji make poetic sense — tomorrow is the day that dawns bright and new. But the reading あした has nothing to do with either 明めい (normally めい/あかるい) or 日にち (normally にち/ひ). It's another 熟字じ訓 — a native Japanese time word that got kanji assigned for meaning, not sound.

明日あした actually has three readings: あした (casual, most common), あす (slightly more formal, used in news and writing), and みょうにち (formal/official, used in announcements). Same kanji, three registers. In daily life, あした is what you use.

明日にちあした何する？ (what are you doing tomorrow?). また明日にちあした (see you tomorrow) — one of the most common partings in Japanese. Two words, no verb needed.

明後日あさって: 明あ (bright/next) + 後あと (after) + 日にち (day). The day after the next day. And the reading あさって is, once again, a native word that ignores the individual kanji sounds.

English doesn't have a single common word for this concept — "the day after tomorrow" is a four-word phrase. Japanese gives it one word, あさって, spoken in four syllables. The fact that it exists as a single word tells you something about how Japanese organizes time: the near future gets named precisely. 明日にちあした (tomorrow), 明後日あさって (day after tomorrow), and even 明あか々後あと日にち (しあさって, three days from now). Each gets its own word. Beyond that, you switch to counting: 四し日にち後あと (four days later).

明後あと日にちあさっては休やすみだ (the day after tomorrow is a day off). A handy word for planning anything — no awkward arithmetic required.

The mirror image of 明後日あさって, looking backward instead of forward. 一昨日おととい: 一いっ (one) + 昨さく (previous) + 日にち (day). And the reading おととい — five syllables of native Japanese that couldn't care less what the kanji say.

The complete near-time lineup now looks like this, centered on today:

一昨日にちきのうおととい (おととい, day before yesterday)
昨日にちきのう (きのう, yesterday)
今日にちきょう (きょう, today)
明日にちあした (あした, tomorrow)
明後あと日にちあさって (あさって, day after tomorrow)

Five days, five words, all with irregular readings. If this seems like a lot of memorization, it is — but they're such high-frequency words that they'll stick fast through sheer repetition. You'll hear おととい whenever someone is talking about recent events: おととい買かったばかりなのに (I just bought it the day before yesterday, and yet...) — a common setup for a complaint about something breaking too soon.
